Below is a **clean lakehouse reference architecture**, explicitly **mapping where AWS Glue, Amazon EMR, and Databricks sit**, *and why*, using the **decision tree logic** you just saw.

This is the version you can use for:

*   Architecture reviews
*   SAP‑C02 elimination questions
*   Enterprise platform design discussions

***

# 1️⃣ Lakehouse Architecture – Big Picture

A **lakehouse** combines:

*   **Data Lake** (S3, open formats, cheap storage)
*   **Warehouse capabilities** (ACID, schema enforcement, performant SQL)
*   **Multiple compute engines** on top of the same data

The key idea:

> **Storage is decoupled from compute**

***

# 2️⃣ End‑to‑End Lakehouse Architecture (Mapped to Glue / EMR / Databricks)

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

# 3️⃣ Exactly Where Each Service Fits (and **why**)

## 🟢 AWS Glue — **Lakehouse Foundation Layer**

**Position:** Ingestion + ETL + Metadata

### What Glue owns

*   Raw → Silver → Gold transformations
*   Schema evolution & partitioning
*   Data Catalog (single source of truth)
*   Serverless batch + basic streaming ETL

### Why Glue is here

Glue aligns with the decision tree:

*   ✅ No cluster management
*   ✅ ETL / data integration
*   ✅ Batch pipelines
*   ✅ Governance-friendly

> **Glue is the backbone of the lakehouse, not the query engine.**

***

## 🔵 Amazon EMR — **Heavy Compute / Engineering Layer**

**Position:** Advanced transformations & cost‑optimized Spark

### What EMR owns

*   Very large joins / aggregations
*   Optimized Spark jobs (memory, shuffle, GC tuning)
*   Compaction jobs for Iceberg/Hudi
*   Long-running or always-on Spark workloads

### Why EMR fits

Decision tree logic:

*   ✅ You want to manage clusters
*   ✅ Fine‑grained Spark control
*   ✅ Cost optimization with Spot / Graviton

> EMR is used **selectively**, not everywhere.

***

## 🟣 Databricks — **Analytics & ML Lakehouse Layer**

**Position:** Advanced analytics + data science + ML

### What Databricks owns

*   Notebook-driven analytics
*   Delta Lake ACID tables
*   Feature engineering
*   ML experimentation (MLflow, feature store)

### Why Databricks fits

Decision tree logic:

*   ✅ Notebooks & collaboration matter
*   ✅ Analytics / ML productivity is key
*   ✅ Lakehouse‑first analytics

> Databricks sits **on top of the same S3 data**, not instead of it.

***

# 4️⃣ Query & Consumption Mapping (Very Important)

| Persona           | Engine              | Why                      |
| ----------------- | ------------------- | ------------------------ |
| Business analysts | Athena              | Serverless, SQL, cheap   |
| BI dashboards     | Redshift / Spectrum | Performance, concurrency |
| Data engineers    | EMR                 | Control, tuning          |
| Data scientists   | Databricks          | Notebooks, ML            |
| Platform team     | Glue Catalog        | Governance               |

This is **multi‑engine, single‑data** lakehouse.

***

# 5️⃣ How This Maps Directly to the Decision Tree

| Decision Tree Question       | Lakehouse Mapping |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------- |
| “Is it ETL?”                 | Glue Jobs         |
| “Do I want cluster control?” | EMR               |
| “Notebooks / ML important?”  | Databricks        |
| “Batch scheduled jobs?”      | Glue              |
| “Heavy continuous Spark?”    | EMR               |

***

# 6️⃣ Exam‑Critical Insight (SAP‑C02 style)

> **Glue builds the lakehouse**\
> **EMR optimizes the lakehouse**\
> **Databricks exploits the lakehouse**

AWS exam questions almost never expect:

*   “Choose only one”

They *do* expect:

*   **Correct placement of each service**

***

# 7️⃣ Common Architectural Mistakes (Seen in Real Reviews)

❌ Using Databricks for basic ingestion\
❌ Using EMR as a platform default for all ETL\
❌ Skipping Glue Catalog and hardcoding S3 paths\
❌ Treating Redshift as lakehouse storage

***
